# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Helm chart for our application deployment without resource limits or requests. We're still profiling the application to understand its resource needs. Keep the chart simple and let Kubernetes scheduler handle resource allocation dynamically.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: v1   # version of api, should be consistent with your helm values file    (e.g., 2018-09-03) or kubectl get command output for installed chart versions at the time of creation in this example
kind: HelmChart        # specifies what kind object to create - Chart here means we are creating a new package from our charts/appchart folder structure. Can be Deployment, StatefulSet etc., based on your requirement (e.g., Kubernetes application)  Or Pods for single pod app
metadata:               # metadata about the chart in k8s format - name & namespace of helm deployment(Chart's release/version). Recommendation is to use a unique value and make it meaningful like 'my-app', or shorten if version based (e.g., myApp) as not clear from context
  name: app    # This should be the same with your chart’s directory on git repo, Helm uses this field for package management in case of rename/move operations and it will change when installing a new instance to prevent duplicate names across different releases. Can also replace 'app' if more clear than version or shorten as per requirement (e.g., myApp)
  namespace: default    # If not defined, Kubernetes uses the "default" space unless otherwise specified by --namespace flag   ('-n', followed by value in parentheses e.g. kubectl -n <space> get pods') if there are multiple namespaces you can set them here (e.g., 'prod' or other)
  labels:           # Labels for selectors, groups & selection algorithm to decide what resources the Kubernetes scheduler uses when scheduling   ('app': app name , '-name/'-namespace-selected apps') in this example `{"foo": "bar", "version":"0.1"}` as defined by Helm values file
    - foo: bar      # Recommendation is to use meaningful labels like 'release', version & environment (e,g., name='v2'), and appname-env ('app': your application'-environment('dev')) based on the chart requirement for easy filtering in Kubernetes logs/events.
    - version: 0.1   # Recommendation is to use meaningful labels like 'release', or shorten as per requirements (e.,g, v2) with a clear context e..g ('version':your-app'-'dev') based on the chart requirement for easy filtering in Kubernetes logs/events
    - app: myApp     # Recommendation is to use meaningful labels like 'release', or shorten as per requirements (e.,g, v2) with a clear context e..g ('version':your-app'-'dev') based on the chart requirement for easy filtering in Kubernetes logs/events
spec:                  # spec defines what our k8s resources look are here. It will depend upon how your app is configured (e., For Single and Replica set). 'replicas', "template" etc can be used depends depending on type of pod you want to create in Kubernetes like StatefulSet, Deployment with values for replica counts/containers 
   replicas: 3         # defines how many instances we would maintain or run based upon our application's requirement e.g., If your app is running multi-instances then set it up as per requirements (e.. g.'5') and can be dynamically increased if required in the future by using 'kubectl scale'.
   template:          # this will depend on how you want to configure container's/pod settings, for instance Pod configuration may have values like image name & port etc. Here is a general example of configuring Kubernetes pod setting with Docker images (can be updated according your need).  ('image': 'my-app:10.2', "ports":[{'containerPort' :3456, 'hostPort'：80}]})
   image:              # specifies the docker imgae to use in case of single pod app like if you want a specific version then provide it as per requirement (example `my-app` or just name - not all apps may have this field set),  ('name': 'yourApp', 'version' : myVersion)
   ports:               # defines what port your application exposes to outside world, e.g., If you want the app exposed on an external IP and if so then provide it in format (containerPort：3456，hostport 80). ('ports': [{'containerPort' :3456 , 'hostPort'：80 }])
